Now he's also the Ritz-Carlton guy, so I've got to keep stretching to make the Ritz-Carlton experience relevant to this particular restaurant owner. I can't get away with just telling him he's done a bunch of Ritz-Carlton stuff. Here's some of our super conference copy. So we got Ross, right? And predominantly, George Ross is negotiating real estate deals for Trump. So our audience is not exclusively made up of people who invest in real estate. So I got to hunt for, I got to tell him, first of all, I got to like insist that he's for every kind of business owner and every entrepreneur because this is prevention. The thing I don't want them to do in their head is, oh, George Ross is in Trump's real estate guy, so he's not relevant to me. And they will do it if left to their own devices. There's one little business thing from Ross's background when he, you know, partner owned radio stations, which gets me him out of real estate and into another environment. So I use that. And if you were going, say, to just a real estate audience, you probably wouldn't even bother mentioning it. If you look at 167, so if we're selling negotiating advice, right, so he only does big deals, right? Well, whether you want to buy a pizza shop or a skyscraper, whether you want to save money on your next car, whether you want to sell at prices unrestricted by competition, this advice is for you. So I'm trying to open doors for people to see relevance where they might otherwise not see it. Then because we're going to use them doing the boardroom deal with the contest, now I get to create relevance by tying him to so you're going to see the people in the contest presenting their greatest marketing and advertising stuff. So I got something to work with there as well. And there are other examples there for you as well. And I guess the point is, is you've got to think very clearly about who you are writing to, where are their heads at, and try and put in front of them everything they will consider relevant, anything you're putting them in front of them that they will not automatically consider relevant, you've got to make relevant, or you've got to make go away. So your entire life story, some pieces of it, are maybe of no use to you because they aren't or can't be made relevant to your audience. So if you sell sales training to a lot of sales people and you figure out that they come from these eight industries, the fact that you were vice president of thus and so in a completely different industry, say that's not particularly helpful to you unless you can stretch and figure out a way to reword it. Because it could actually invalidate you, always one of them corporate guys from a Fortune 500 and we're not, so you've always got to sweat, what are they doing with this fact that's not helpful to you, and how can I make this fact helpful to me and relevant to them in the way I want it to be. Next principle, 169. That's a pretty important one. I call it the principle of welcome profit status. It really has to do with source. And it fundamentally says that most of what we is profitable for us to do in writing to the influence people is not change their minds. But instead to give them what they want to appear as the profit they hoped would appear, not as the profit they don't want. Unwelcome advice is a ugly grimy business. About the only time that it pays to do it is after you have somebody who has ascended in a relationship or maybe in a private client environment. But in terms of numbers, the sale of unwelcome advice is a losing proposition. People will simply go, they'll shop for the advice they like until they find somebody giving them advice they like and they'll give him all their money. And so you won't really keep a lot of people around by ramming down their throats what they resist. Well furthermore, let me take it further. If you were to stand in, say, the weight loss section and watch people buy books all day, you would see a lot of people avoiding books that are on the fitness end of that shelf where it is very clear that they involve exercise. You would see other people gravitate to them. So it depends now on what your bias is. We used to let Ted Brewer with his 10 foods never to eat. The only place he just bombed with that was Alabama. And I mean the first time we were there in Birmingham and his sales numbers were terrible. And he's like fussing afterwards. I can't figure out what went wrong. That's a tit. You tell them they can't drink any soda pop. You tell them they can't eat moon pies. And you tell them they can't eat burgers. You just eliminated the whole damn diet. Furthermore, the arena we're in is in the shadow of the Coca-Cola plant. Which you know, this is a Coca-Cola town. This is their diet. They're sitting on the folding chair in front of the gas station eating a cheeseburger, a moon pie and drinking a doctor pepper. I mean, what did you expect? You know? I mean you can't do it. I actually did get them to modify. We didn't go there very often. But Rhode Island, Massachusetts and stuff. I mean one of the things he really beats up is lobsters. Can't eat lobsters. Religious reasons and they're the cockroaches of the sea and on and on and on. You know, which they are. You know, that's what they are. They're giant cockroaches. And by the way, Ted's not a guy you want to like go out to dinner with. Made that mistake once. You're pretty much down to water. You know? And you're going to have a conversation about that. But I mean, I said, you know, hey Ted, you know, we're in Boston. You better lay off the lobster damn deal today because, you know, that's like what the place is all about. So that day he did the nine foods never to eat. But I mean, you got to think about this. You know, there's, I mean, and so to have total integrity, we got to deliver some information to people that they're not immediately receptive to. But you better pick your time and your place, you know, and you better be careful about the way that you do it. They just had a thing in the Ohio State Michigan game. They had this big special on HBO, but this is the game of the year every year. And they had a clip of John Currie where he did like an appearance in Cleveland in the morning and says, yeah, go Buckeyes. And he flies to Taylor, Michigan. I thought it was right. He forgets where he is. And he's like, I'm for Buckeye football. And the one guy, you know, the one guy in the background go, you know anything. He's still in Ohio and he's in Michigan. Well, we could make a, we could make a list of Currie's, you know.
